sentences.text: Hyman Flancbaum.
H-Y-M-A-N F-L-A-N-C-B-A-U-M.
June the 20th, 1920.
I was born in Poland, the city Wachock.
W-A-C-H-O-C-K.
A very small Jewish population.
The town wasn't so small, just Jewish was maybe 100 families.
Very small town.
We were a minority.
Skarzysko-Kamienna, Radom, [?
Voivodship, ?] Kielce.
My father's name was Smuel.
My mother's name was Chawa.
We were five children.
My older sister was Freida Flancbaum-- Freida.
My brother was Velvua.
He's not alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Da-- David Flancbaum comes the third, which he is alive.
And Sarah Flancbaum was killed.
And I am the fifth one.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Yes, I went to school.
I went to heder.
I went to school-- to public school.
We-- I woke up at, like, 7:00. 8:00, I had to be in the public school.
From 8:00 to 1:00, we were in public school.
After 1:00, I went to heder until 6:00.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't enjoy too much public school.
The reason, the antisemitism was very big.
Catholics-- most of the people were Catholics and they were-- and they hated Jews.
When I-- when I was in school-- I don't remember which class when I was, either the fourth or the fifth grade.
It was an incident which I had with a-- with a neighbor, with my own neighbor.
He was-- was close-- it was a close neighbor to where I used to live.
He gave me always trouble.
Many-- many times I had to bring him five grosz in order to bribe him, he should leave me alone.
Didn't help.
Didn't help.
Always he hit me, he made fun out of me.
Not only himself, but a lot of them.
But he was the worst guy.
And one day I said, I have to take revenge.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was a very bad day for me.
I did it.
I was sorry, but I had to do it.
He was sitting-- we-- we-- our class was on the first floor, and he was sitting by a window.
Fighting, I know I'm going to lose.
Then I-- I throwed him down through the window, and he broke a leg.
The principal came over to me and he said, why did you do that?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I told him the truth.
This goes on for months, for [INAUDIBLE] more than years.
He bothered me, and I told my teacher and she didn't take-- she didn't take any action.
I had to do it myself.
I did it.
The principal asked me, are you sorry?
I said, of course I'm sorry.
Not only I'm sorry, when I'm going to come home, I'm going-- my father-- my father going to beat me up why I did it.
Later he said I should go home and call my parents.
And my parents had to come to the school to explain the whole situation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
It happened the teacher didn't punish me.
I don't know why.
I explained myself real good and [INAUDIBLE].
But I felt-- I felt sorry myself for why I did it.
And I had-- my father was-- should rest in peace-- yelled at me why I did it.
And I explained him 1,000 times.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And finally, I mean, he had to-- he had to accept it.
I explained myself why I did it, why I had no other choice.
And finally I got peace in the school a little bit.
But I didn't-- should I be honest with you?
I never enjoyed the school because we were-- we were a few kids, Jewish kids in the school.
We were 33 children in the school.
I mean, maybe three or four-- three or four Jewish kids.
The luck is I was a very good student in math.
I was a very-- number one student in math.
I was very good.
And everybody looked up at me because if they-- if we had a test, I couldn't sit in my seat.
I was to go up to the-- where the teacher was, his place, and I was-- let's say if it took-- it's supposed to take 45 minutes, the test, I made it in 30 minutes.
And I had to walk out from the store-- from the house because the teacher was afraid that I'm going to help somebody else.
We were doing-- we weren't too rich home.
Wasn't a-- hardly we made a living.
We-- my-- we bought pieces of goods and materials, and we sold them.
It was on the market, like a-- like a flea market.
And this was our [NON-ENGLISH].
I mean, that was our livelihood.
No, my mother was took care of the children.
My mother never worked.
The only thing is my brother-- one-- my-- I had two brothers, very capable fellows, and they helped a lot to bring food to the house, like making money.
My older brother was a tailor and he was very, very good.
He was a good-- we were a very close family, very close family.
One for another would give their life.
And the middle brother was exceptionally good, David, which is my buddy now.
He's alive, thank God.
He's older than me.
He was a very, very fine man, fine-- fine kid.
Orthodox, yes.
Shabbat was a very great day to us.
Friday, my-- my mother used to light candles.
We-- me and my father, had to [INAUDIBLE] we were three brothers.
We went to shul for Shabbos services.
We came home.
My father made kiddish.
Every child had to make kiddish in the house.
We were singing [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a beautiful, beautiful-- beautiful Shabbos.
We were waiting for that Shabbos.
We were so enjoyed it, we were singing.
All had good voices.
We were wor-- we were living-- how should I describe you-- not in the front of the house, just in the back of the house.
And all our neighbors came in hearing how we were singing [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what [NON-ENGLISH] is?
Yes.
And we enjoy ourselves.
And Shabbos in the morning-- that was Saturday night.
Shabbos in the morning, we went all three with my father to-- to shul.
He was praying, and after the shul we came home.
My mother prepared, for-- for the whole family, all kinds of foods.
And the same thing.
We were singing [NON-ENGLISH].
And my father used to tell us how, when he was in the army in the-- watchamacallit-- in the, I think the Russian army, I don't even remember where.
He'd tell us stories for [INAUDIBLE], all kinds of stories.
And we had a poor life.
I mean, this was our social life.
The Shabbos was a great thing in our house.
It was a beautiful, beautiful thing.
And my sisters was helping us singing.
We were a very, very close family.
We enjoyed Shabbos.
It was the biggest day of our-- Shabbos was like a real holidays.
We-- we were waiting the whole week for Shabbos.
Yes.
We had an uncle.
We had an aunt, my mo-- my mother's sister.
She used to live not far from us.
We used to visit her, like, I would say, at least once a week.
We went to her, like, on the Shabbos.
After-- after-- after Shabbos, we used to go to the aunt.
And we were sitting by the aunt and [INAUDIBLE], she made her coffee.
And when she come to us, we made her coffee-- like, not the coffee.
It was tea.
Tea, mostly.
It was tea.
And this was our life.
Yes, I had cousins.
Which they were-- we were very close, very close.
And we-- and that's the way we spend our days.
Too much goals we couldn't have-- we couldn't, because we could never-- I could never go out of town to a high school because, first of all, it was very expensive.
They didn't accept Jewish-- like, Jews in the high-- in the high school.
And it was very, very expensive.
And besides everything, my father would never allow me to do that because I have to travel.
And I could never dream to travel on Shabbos, [INAUDIBLE] Shabbos.
This was-- this was a very great sin, and I wouldn't have the guts to ask my parents that they should allow me to do this.
I respect my parents.
My parents had a chair-- my father has a chair in the house.
Nobody would be able to sit on this chair.
Only my father.
My mother has a place.
Everyone had a place in our house, and I would never take my brother's place, and my brother would never take my place.
And from the father and mother, there's no question about it.
This was their place, designated place, and no-- nobody would have to-- this is-- this is Daddy's place.
That was in our house.
Very close family.
Very small place.
We was li-- we-- we were seven people-- five and-- and with my father and mother, seven.
I used to sleep with my brother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my brother-- we were three brothers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my elder brother used to sleep with the-- with the father.
We were two-- we were-- there-- there were two sisters that were sleeping together.
Mommy would sleep alone.
Very small place.
I wouldn't be able to-- to youth-- to use [INAUDIBLE] organization, because we had a little organization-- a Zionist organization, and my father-- should rest in peace-- said, this is [INAUDIBLE].
Don't go in there, because you will learn good things there.
And I couldn't go there.
But I sympathize with them.
I like-- I would love to go there, but I couldn't go on account of my parents.
I used to go-- mostly I'd spend my time in shul, learning and-- and-- and talk about politics, what's going to happen.
I liked politics very much.
I like-- I like-- I used to read the Jewish paper.
I used to read the Polish paper.
I used to like to do it.
Jewish books, yes.
Yes.
Affect my life?
I'm going to give you a little story.
In 1935, I had, I remember a teacher.
She told me-- we had-- when-- when Pilsudski passed away-- maybe you heard of Pilsudski?
He was the prime minister of Poland.
And we had to wear a armband, a black armband.
And then one day I forgot to wear it, not because I didn't want to wear it, because happened I liked that man, which he passed away, because he was a Jewish friend, Pilsudski.
Then my teacher said to me one day when I was not wearing his-- if Hitler would pass away, you would wear this.
And I said yes, you're right, but I just forgot about it, to do it.
Not I didn't-- not-- unconsciously, I forgot.
I didn't-- I didn't want this-- she gave me a few days after this I didn't hear from her.
She always criticized me why I didn't wear that armband.
Hitler was-- when we-- when we-- when we were talking in heder with our rabbi, the rabbi used to talk about that, this [NON-ENGLISH].
It's coming-- a [NON-ENGLISH].
How should I explain this in English?
It's coming-- a very, very bad time, for-- especially for Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's growing-- Hitler's growing a cancer.
We knew about it's going to be bad, but we didn't-- we didn't exactly know it's going to be so bad.
We, like in 1935, '36, '37, we was learning history in the school about the danger of-- of the fascism, in school even, in-- in this-- in Polish school.
And our teacher explained to us that he wants to conquer the world.
I mean, I didn't know about the world.
I knew only about Europe.
I didn't know of America at that time.
We-- we-- we were very frightened, but we had no-- to go to Israel, I couldn't dream about it.
My older brother went for a [NON-ENGLISH].
You know-- you know-- how to explain this?
He wanted to go to Israel illegally, then he has to train himself in a little town.
And he wanted, but he-- let's say, if the war wouldn't-- come 1939, maybe he wouldn't be able to go.
Like a-- like a certificate-- how do you call that?
Syndicate that he would go to Israel.
He-- let's say he had to work about three, four years in order to earn this, my older brother.
Then he would have saved himself, but he didn't-- he didn't make it.
I know a lot of things, but I was helpless.
I couldn't do anything about it.
We were talking-- we were friends in school.
We never realized that he is going to be so powerful, to conquer the whole world.
We were brainwashed by the Polish-- by the Polish principles.
We are not afraid.
We wouldn't give even a button of them.
We're going to fight him.
We're going to win the war.
And the war only took maybe a few-- a few days.
He conquered our town in a few days.
I mean, when this-- in 1939, my brother was called to the army but he didn't make it because [INAUDIBLE].
This was a very bad day, because all our neighbors get together, like a night before, and they said we're going to leave this little town.
Because we were in a town between-- Starachowice was a big ammunition factory, and Skarzysko was a big ammunition factory-- Polish factory.
And they-- they said that-- that they-- that the first thing they're going to bomb those two factories.
Then all my little town, a few neighbors who went to-- to another-- to another little town-- they called it [INAUDIBLE].
And over there was terrible.
The big fight [INAUDIBLE] over there.
The-- we arrived that time on a Friday.
I remember just like now.
This was maybe September-- September the 1st when the war broke out.
Maybe September the 4th we came to that little town, and over there was a big fight between the Polish army and the German army.
Didn't last too long.
Overnight was over, but we-- we had a terrible, bitter, bitter pill there, and we had to go back.
When we came back to our town, the whole town was burned down.
One Pollock-- when the-- when the German army came into our little town, one Pollock went on the roof and start shooting.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then the whole German took four corners of the little town and burned it down.
Then we had no place to go.
Then we all went-- we had a big synagogue.
Then we slept over at the synagogue two or three nights, the whole town.
And after that, everybody, my father-- should he rest in peace-- rented a room, another place with another family together, not by ourselves, like partners.
He would enter this apartment with another couple.
We lived there maybe not too long, because they pushed us to-- they made a ghetto.
After 1939, a very short time, maybe-- I don't remember how long this took, and they give us, like, four or five blocks-- streets-- and all the Jews had to live in one place.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They give us a room, I remember like now.
And we all lived.
And my-- my older sister got just married before the war, and she was pregnant.
I don't remember when she had the baby.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was right after-- in middle.
I mean, like 1940, and we were already in the-- the ghetto.
Yeah.
Yes.
The ghetto was like that.
Let's say we had to, like, say, like we woke up in the morning.
They took me to work.
I used to work for a German firm.
I don't remember exactly the firm.
I think it was [?
Stuart. ?] We used to go work on roads.
We made roads.
And about 5:00 6:00 was the curfew.
We had to be home.
And this job, let's say, like, it would start 8:00 to 4:00, and after 4:00, I think, got to get home.
And that was my work.
Very poor.
Very, very, poor.
Very difficult to live.
To make a living was very hard.
My father couldn't go anymore because he had a beard.
Had to cut his beard one day because this way, maybe they wouldn't recognize him or something.
And my older-- my older brother and my younger brother brought in to make a living, for-- for the entire family.
It was very difficult to live, very terrible condition.
Very, very bad.
No.
No schools.
Synagogue, we made a-- [INAUDIBLE] in the back.
We had a room, and all the neighbors get together, like, Friday night for Shabbos, the morning to pray.
We had no [?
Sefer. ?] We had no, but we-- we-- we prayed in the book by a [?
Hadish. ?] And that's the way we were living.
We had-- we had no shul.
The shul was taken away from-- they made like a-- for cows, for horses.
[INAUDIBLE] no shul.
We had no shul.
We had no schools.
Nothing.
Nothing.
The only thing when-- when-- no, I'll tell you, no.
No, it was nothing.
In 19-- this was til 1942.
In 1942, we heard rumors that they're taking people to work.
We didn't know that they're taking people to kill them.
We knew that they're taking people to go to work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we heard that this little town was taken away, this one, this one.
I remember in Rosh Hashanah when my teacher-- he was a pray for-- he used to pray for [INAUDIBLE].
I mean, he was the prayer, like a-- like a cantor.
Then he, when it came to [INAUDIBLE], he would start crying.
I walked over to that rabbi, to that-- to that-- I said, [NON-ENGLISH], Rabbi, why do you cry?
I say, [INAUDIBLE].
It's coming.
It's getting very, very dangerous, and very bad.
Our next hand will be-- we're going to have to go.
And that was Rosh Hashanah and this was October.
I don't know what month that was, Rosh Hashanah at that month.
But it was very close.
And it took us maybe three or four weeks.
They called all our town.
Everyone should come on the marketplace, on like a-- how do you call this place?
Like a-- like a big park.
No, it's not-- it wasn't a park.
Just a big place.
And every Jew had to come out and we couldn't even take nothing.
We had to leave-- I mean, actually we left everything in the house.
We walked out to that-- to that park, and they made a selection.
They selected people-- young people, older people, middle-aged people, children separately.
And I was staying with my father.
We were staying with my father.
Me, my brother, and my older brother.
And my father said the last word to us, children, where are you leaving me?
We couldn't explain ourselves.
We couldn't kiss each other goodbye.
Left and right and we had to go away.
And that's it.
That day they took us-- in day or night, I don't remember.
They-- they were holding us on a-- on a-- on a big field.
We slept overnight there in that place.
In the morning, next morning, they took us to the ammunitions factory, Hermann Goering's [INAUDIBLE].
This was Starachowice.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from that day on, from 1940 to-- from October-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] stop?
